summary: Hotz discusses his purchase of 123 acres in California with the goal of building an alternative to the university system, but discovers that managing physical infrastructure is fundamentally harder than software because of entropy. Unlike software which can be reverted and automated with tools, physical systems require constant maintenance that doesn't get easier over time. He argues that the professional managerial class derives its power from managing these entropy-fighting human systems. The solution, he believes, is AI and robotics - robotic plumbers and robot-fixers that can maintain themselves with only energy and raw materials as inputs. He's shelving the land project for now because it can't sustain itself without his labor, but sees comma.ai's self-driving work as the first step toward general-purpose robots that will eventually make such projects possible.
key_points: Software is 'tool complete' and resistant to regression - you can always revert it - while physical systems constantly decay due to entropy
The professional managerial class derives its power from managing the human systems that fight entropy in the physical world
True automation requires closing the loop: robotic workers plus robotic robot-fixers, with only energy and raw materials as inputs
The 'management coefficient' concept - how many people someone can manage - is the limiting factor on organizational growth
Self-driving cars are just the start; the real goal is home robotics, then general-purpose robots for building, plumbing, and self-repair
money_quotes: Software has this beautiful way of being tool complete and much harder to regress than physical things. You can always revert software.
Consider going to a well to fetch water. Every day, you have to do that. And every day you do it, it doesn't become easier.
Entropy is constantly chipping away at it, in a way that it doesn't chip away at software.
This is considered 'blue-collar' work, but it's managed by much more complex systems than those that manage software.
Robotic plumbers. And while that still doesn't complete the tool hierarchy, just one more thing does. Robotic robot fixers.
With that, energy and raw material enters, and entropy is maintained.
A paradise built and maintained by machines.
key_insight: The fundamental advantage of software over physical systems is its immunity to entropy, and the path to sustainable physical infrastructure requires closing the automation loop with self-repairing robots.
